_metadata.description: Productivity gain estimates from real-world Claude conversations for the Live Data Dashboard (Component 4A). Contains occupation-level time/cost/savings data, the distribution of time savings across tasks, specific task examples, and economy-wide productivity implications. Structured for Practice Activity P4 (Dashboard Exploration: Productivity Distributions).

primary_source.title: Estimating AI Productivity Gains from Claude Conversations
primary_source.sample: 100,000 Claude.ai conversations (Free, Pro, and Max tiers)
primary_source.wage_data: Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) May 2024, U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics
primary_source.task_taxonomy: O*NET Database, U.S. Department of Labor

critical_caveats: Time savings estimates do NOT account for additional time humans spend refining Claude's outputs outside the chat window, iterating across multiple sessions, or validating accuracy. Actual real-world productivity gains are likely lower than these estimates.

Past randomized controlled trials have found smaller time savings: 56%, 40%, 26%, 14%, and even negative time savings across different applications (p. 13). These differences may reflect earlier model generations or the additional refinement time not captured in transcript analysis.
The 1.8% annual productivity figure assumes universal adoption of current-generation AI across the entire US economy over 10 years — it is an extrapolation exercise, not a prediction.
Claude's time estimates show a compression bias: overestimating short tasks and underestimating long ones. Actual variation in task lengths may be larger than reported (p. 8, 10).
_metadata.cross_document_note: The body text on p. 12 states 'the median conversation experienced an estimated 84% time savings.' The Figure 6 caption on p. 13 states 'the overall median savings is 81%.' These measure slightly different things (conversation-level median vs. task-level median). Per cross-document review (process journal, Week of April 23, 2026), 81% from the Figure 6 caption is the canonical figure used across all portfolio deliverables.

aggregate_metrics.average_time_reduction_source: Overview, p. 2 — 'Claude speeds up individual tasks by about 80%'
aggregate_metrics.median_task_cost_source: p. 10 — 'Claude handles work that would cost a median of $54 in professional labor'

occupation_table.description: Claude's estimates by SOC major group. Time without AI is the estimated hours a human professional would need. Average wage is from OEWS May 2024. Task cost is computed by multiplying median task time by hourly wage, weighted by task prevalence. Time savings are computed as 1 - time_with_ai / time_without_ai.

time_savings_distribution.description: Distribution of time savings across O*NET tasks. The density plot shows an uneven distribution concentrated in the 50–95% range with a peak between 80–90%. This distribution is the key data for P4 — it shows learners that AI productivity gains are not uniform.
time_savings_distribution.range_description: Most tasks fall between 50% and 95% time savings

annotated_examples.explanation: Already a task that can be done quickly by experts without AI assistance
Hardware-related tasks see lower time savings
Software development tasks cluster near the median range
AI systems can read, extract, and cite information much more quickly than people

rct_comparison.description: Past randomized controlled trials have found smaller time savings than Claude's self-estimates, suggesting the additional refinement work not captured in transcript analysis substantially reduces net productivity gains.
rct_comparison.note: One RCT found negative time savings. These studies examined earlier model generations.

example_tasks.description: Nine specific task examples from Figure 4 showing the range of time, cost, and savings across occupations. These concrete examples help learners anchor abstract productivity data to recognizable workplace tasks.

tasks.task: Develop curricula and plan course content and methods of instruction
Compile bibliographies of specialized materials for outside reading assignments
Troubleshoot problems involving office equipment, such as computer hardware and software
Prepare tables, graphs, fact sheets, and written reports summarizing research results
Prepare invoices, reports, memos, letters, financial statements, and other documents
Advise students on academic and vocational curricula and on career issues
Interpret data on price, yield, stability, future investment-risk trends, economic influences, and other factors affecting investment programs

tasks.occupation: Educational, Guidance, School, and Vocational Counselors
tasks.task: Provide students with information on topics such as college degree programs and admission requirements, financial aid opportunities, trade and technical schools
Compose, type, and distribute meeting notes, routine correspondence, or reports

economy_wide_impact.description: Extrapolation of task-level productivity gains to economy-wide impact using Hulten's theorem. Assumes universal adoption of current-generation AI across the US economy over 10 years.
economy_wide_impact.methodology: Hulten's theorem — weighted average of task-level productivity gains, following Acemoglu (2024) baseline approach. Each task weighted by (i) fraction of occupation's time spent on that task and (ii) occupation's share of total US wage bill.

top_contributing_occupations.occupation: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists

economy_wide_impact.low_contributing_sectors: Restaurants, healthcare delivery, construction, and retail contribute much less — mostly because few of their tasks appear in the conversation data.

bottleneck_concept.description: As AI accelerates some tasks, the remaining tasks where AI provides less speedup become a larger share of the occupation's work. These 'bottleneck tasks' may constrain overall productivity gains.

examples.bottleneck: Coordinating system installation, supervising other engineers

validation_summary.description: Summary of the validation methodology and key findings. Relevant to the dashboard as context for how confident learners should be in the productivity estimates.

self_consistency.method: Multiple prompt variations tested on 1,800 consented conversations
self_consistency.interpretation: Estimates are stable across prompt phrasing

external_benchmarking.method: Tested against JIRA tickets for open-source repositories with developer estimates and tracked completion times (1,000 tasks)
external_benchmarking.interpretation: Claude's estimates have similar directional correlation to developer self-estimates, though Claude significantly overestimates short tasks and underestimates long ones.

validation_summary.compression_bias: Claude's time estimates are more compressed than reality — predicting comparatively long times for shorter tasks and vice versa. This means actual differences in task lengths may be larger than reported.
